name: juno (fellow capitoline)
shortDescription: Juno, queen of the gods and wife of Jupiter, was a powerful deity in Roman mythology. She was the protector of marriage, women, and childbirth, and one of the Capitoline Triad.
description: Juno, as the queen of the gods in Roman mythology, held a prominent position of power and influence. Wife to Jupiter, the king of the gods, she was the protector and special counselor of the state. Juno was believed to watch over women and marriage. Her Greek equivalent is Hera. As Juno Regina, she was a member of the Capitoline Triad (along with Jupiter and Minerva) venerated on the Capitoline Hill in Rome. Juno's origins are shrouded in the mists of time, and she was associated with the Etruscan goddess Uni. She was seen as the embodiment of female vitality and generative power. Her major festivals included the Matronalia (March 1st), dedicated to married women, and the Larentalia. Temples dedicated to her were built throughout the Roman world, including the Temple of Juno Moneta on the Capitoline. Myths surrounding Juno often depict her as a jealous and vengeful figure, particularly towards Jupiter's lovers and illegitimate children. However, she was also revered for her strength, wisdom, and protective qualities, especially for Roman women and the state.
